FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=10,054, Year 3 (2019–2021), ages 12–13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Parental screen time practices (bedroom screen use, mealtime screen use, parental monitoring) constitute smartphone/screen-based device use exposure; applies B1 (YSTS papers reporting separate coefficients for distinct screen behaviors)

FT_IC3.evidence: Ordinal logistic regression models with adjusted odds ratios for associations between media parenting practices and R-rated movies/mature video games

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios (95% confidence intervals) reported in Table 2, e.g., AOR 1.44 [1.36–1.53] for bedroom screen use and R-rated movies

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis examining associations between parental media practices and adolescent mature-content consumption

FT_EC7_reason: Paper examines media parenting practices (screen use) as PREDICTORS of R-rated movie and mature game consumption (outcomes), not demographics predicting smartphone/SM use. Fails condition (a): smartphone/SM is not the sole dependent variable.
notes: Exposes qualifying smartphone variables (screen-based device use) as independent variables predicting mature content outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=9,519, baseline (2016–2018, ages 9–10) to Year 3 (2019–2021, ages 12–13)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time disaggregated into TV shows/movies, video games (single/multi-player), and digital socializing (texting, video chatting, social media); qualifies as smartphone/app use and social media variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Compositional data analysis using mixed-effects models with time as continuous independent variable; isometric log-ratio transformations and bootstrap confidence intervals

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 and Figure 1 report mean hours/day with 95% confidence intervals for screen time, sleep, sports/exercise, and other activities across four assessments

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis of ABCD Study data examining changes in screen time and related behaviors

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (smartphone/social media use) is the OUTCOME/dependent variable, not the sole predictor. Age and pandemic period are primary independent variables. Fails condition (a): smartphone/SM examined as outcome, not predictor of demographics.
notes: Longitudinal trends analyzed for three separate screen time categories with sex-stratified results showing differential patterns by gender (boys: higher video game increase; girls: higher digital socializing increase).

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, N=2,749, Years 2, 3, and 4 follow-up data (Mage=11.9 at Year 2)

FT_IC2.evidence: Gaming duration and problematic gaming (VGAQ) measured at Year 2; gaming qualifies as smartphone/app use exposure

FT_IC3.evidence: Causal mediation analysis using R package for mediation; reports indirect effects (gaming→caffeine→sleep), direct effects, and total effects with bootstrap confidence intervals

FT_IC4.evidence: Figure 1 displays path coefficients with 95% confidence intervals for indirect effect of caffeine (e.g., indirect effect = 0.023 [0.004, 0.049] for gaming hours→sleep difficulties)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical mediation analysis of ABCD Study data examining caffeine as mediator in gaming-sleep relationship

FT_EC7_reason: Gaming is the primary exposure/independent variable (not outcome). Sleep is the outcome. Caffeine is the mediator. Not a demographics→smartphone/SM structure. Fails condition (a): smartphone/SM not sole dependent variable; gaming is predictor.
notes: Mediation analysis demonstrates caffeine partially mediates relationship between gaming and sleep; indirect effects are small but statistically significant.
